text: This instructional video depicts the surgical management of benign prostatic hyperplasia with robotic-assisted laparoscopic simple supraphibic prostatectomy. Our patient is a seventy-year-old male with a long history of lower urinary tract symptoms, in addition to urinary retention and bladder stones. Physical exam revealed a very large non-tender prostate without nodules. His post-void residual is a hundred cc's. Creatinine is one point O and PSA eighteen point five with a high serum four case score. Marked here is the PI-RADS III lesion in the right medial peripheral zone on MRI fusion biopsy. Prostate size was estimated at two hundred seven grams. Biopsy revealed no evidence of malignancy. CAT scan shows extent of his bladder stones and prostatic enlargement. Several weeks after his biopsy, he was seen in the emergency room for bilateral flank pain. CAT scan showed bilateral hydronephrosis. He was taken to the operating room for ureteral stents.

After discussing various treatment options, the patient decided to undergo a robotic simple suprapubic prostatectomy with removal of his bladder stones. Optimal candidates for simple prostatectomy is generally reserved for prostates larger than one hundred grams. The suprapubic approach is well suited for patients of concurrent bladder stones or diverticula. Minimally invasive methods utilizing robotic assisted surgery has become commonplace.

The surgeon begins the procedure by placing ports, positioning the patient in a modified dorsal lithotomy steep Trendelenburg position. The robotic system is positioned between the patient's legs, with Maryland bipolar forceps in the left robotic arm, monopolar scissors in the right arm, and tenaculum forceps in the third arm. The sigmoid colon is visible below, with the outline of the bladder ahead.

The surgeon makes incisions lateral to the left and right medial umbilical ligaments, followed by transection of the medial umbilical ligaments and urachus. The space of Retzius is developed using blunt dissection and cautery, revealing the pubic bone, bladder, and prostate. The Foley catheter is manipulated to identify the bladder neck, and the bladder is opened transversely approximately three centimeters above the bladder neck using monopolar cautery. The tenaculum forceps are used to retract the anterior lip of the bladder wall, continuing the incision until reaching the lateral limits of the prostate. Bladder stones are encountered and removed using a specimen retrieval bag through the eleven millimeter assistant port.

Five bladder stones are removed using a specimen retrieval bag. The surgeon uses the tenaculum to identify the posterior bladder neck and begins dissection between the prostate adenoma and prostatic capsule. Bipolar and monopolar cautery are used to control bleeding while extending the dissection plane circumferentially. The prostatic adenoma is visibly separating from the capsule with the aid of blunt dissection and retraction.

The surgeon moves the dissection anteriorly after reaching the posterior and lateral limits. A 0 Vicryl suture on a CT needle is placed on the anterior bladder as a holding stitch, which is brought through the anterior abdominal wall using a fascial closure device. The dissection continues anteriorly around the prostate, with the adenoma nearly free. Once completely freed, the adenoma is placed in a specimen retrieval bag and removed in two pieces.

The surgeon uses microsutures to control the pedicles at five and seven o'clock positions. A two-oh microsuture is used to approximate the urethra to the trigone of the bladder with a surgical slip knot, placing a total of five interrupted sutures. The urethral stents are removed, and a twenty-two French three-way Foley catheter is inserted. The bladder is closed in two layers using running two-oh Vicryl sutures, with the first layer demonstrated. The bladder is filled with two hundred cc's of normal saline, showing no evidence of leakage, and the second layer is completed.

The surgeon places a closed suction device and removes all trocars under laparoscopic visualization. The assistant port is closed if necessary, and the specimen is removed. The skin and fascia are then closed.

Robotic time was a hundred and sixty-four minutes, total operative time one hundred and ninety-eight minutes, and estimated blood loss eight hundred and fifty milliliters. Continuous bladder irrigation is ran overnight and clamped on post-op day one. He was kept on clear liquids until passage of flatus. Close suction drain was removed after fluid creatinine was consistent with serum creatinine. He was discharged on post-op day number two. The patient is seen postoperatively in one week and was without complaints. Cystogram showed no leak and the catheter was removed. He will follow up in three months with a post-void residual, blood work, and renal ultrasound. Final pathology showed BPH tissue without evidence of malignancy. We have performed twenty-one robotic simple prostatectomies. Median prostate size is a hundred and seventy-seven grams. Median robotic time is a hundred and sixty-five minutes with an estimated blood loss of four hundred and fifty cc's. Median stay in the hospital is one point five days. There have been a total of four complications all Clavian II. All patients are emptying adequately without incontinence and we have not had any bladder neck contractures. Robotic simple prostatectomy provides good patient outcomes with low morbidity.
This concludes our instructional video on robotic simple suprapubic prostatectomy.
